auditor: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) - Technical Accuracy Auditor

findings.title: --output-format json is not a verified Claude Code CLI flag
findings.description: The module presents `--output-format json` as a valid Claude Code CLI flag in a CI/CD example. Claude Code CLI uses `--output-format` but the exact flag name and accepted values should be verified against current documentation. The flag `--output-format json` may be valid in current versions but was not present in earlier Claude Code releases; this warrants explicit documentation verification since the course has zero-tolerance rules about CLI flag accuracy.
findings.line_context: claude -p "Review this pull request for security issues in src/. Output a structured JSON report." --output-format json
findings.correction: Verify against current Claude Code CLI documentation. If the flag is valid, add a note that this is a real CLI flag. The current Claude Code CLI does support `--output-format` with values like `json`, `text`, `stream-json`, so this is likely correct but should be explicitly verified given the course's zero-tolerance stance.

findings.title: MCP config file referenced as claude_desktop_config.json in Module 05
findings.description: Module 05 references 'claude_desktop_config.json' as the configuration file for MCP server setup. This is the Claude Desktop configuration file, not the Claude Code configuration. Claude Code uses `settings.json` (at `~/.claude/settings.json` or `.claude/settings.json`) or the `claude mcp add` CLI command for MCP server configuration. Module 02 correctly uses settings.json for MCP config, creating an internal inconsistency.
findings.line_context: Configuration in Claude Code's `claude_desktop_config.json` or project-level settings:
findings.correction: Replace 'claude_desktop_config.json' with 'settings.json' (either `~/.claude/settings.json` for global or `.claude/settings.json` for project-level). The `claude_desktop_config.json` file is specific to the Claude Desktop application, not Claude Code CLI.

findings.title: MCP handshake description omits initialized notification
findings.description: The MCP connection handshake in Module 05 describes three phases: initialize, tools/list, and 'Available'. The actual MCP specification handshake includes an `initialized` notification sent by the client after receiving the server's initialize response, before capability listing. The 'Phase 3: Available' is not a protocol-defined phase name but a conceptual description of the post-handshake state.
findings.line_context: **Phase 1: initialize** ... **Phase 2: tools/list** ... **Phase 3: Available** After the handshake completes, the connection is active.
findings.correction: Add the `initialized` notification step between Phase 1 and Phase 2. The correct sequence is: (1) Client sends `initialize` request, server responds with capabilities; (2) Client sends `initialized` notification to confirm; (3) Client sends `tools/list`, `resources/list`, `prompts/list` to discover capabilities. Phase 3 as described is conceptually fine but is not a protocol phase.

findings.title: Sampling described as client-side primitive but without sufficient precision
findings.description: Module 02 mentions 'the MCP specification also defines client-side primitives such as sampling' in a parenthetical note. This is directionally correct -- Sampling is indeed a client-side capability in MCP (the server requests the client to perform LLM sampling). However, the brief mention could be misread as sampling being a separate primitive category. In the MCP spec, Sampling is a client capability that servers can request, not a 'primitive' in the same sense as Tools/Resources/Prompts.
findings.line_context: Note: the MCP specification also defines client-side primitives such as sampling; the list above focuses on what servers expose directly.
findings.correction: The note is acceptable as-is for a foundational course. If precision is desired, rephrase to: 'The MCP specification also defines client capabilities such as sampling (where a server can request the client's LLM to generate text); the list above focuses on the three server-side primitives.'

findings.title: ReAct paper citation venue slightly imprecise
findings.description: Module 01 cites the ReAct paper as 'ICLR 2023' which is correct. Module 02 cites it as 'International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)' without the year. Both are acceptable but should be consistent.
findings.line_context: Yao, S., Zhao, J., Yu, D., Du, N., Shafran, I., Narasimhan, K., & Cao, Y. (2023). ReAct: Synergizing reasoning and acting in language models. *ICLR 2023*.
findings.correction: Minor inconsistency. Both citations are substantively correct. For consistency, use 'International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR), 2023' in both locations.

findings.title: Wei et al. CoT paper venue is correct
findings.description: The Wei et al. (2022) Chain-of-Thought paper is correctly cited as NeurIPS 35 (2022) with the correct arXiv ID 2201.11903. The citation is accurate.
findings.line_context: Wei, J., Wang, X., Schuurmans, D., Bosma, M., Ichter, B., Xia, F., Chi, E., Le, Q., & Zhou, D. (2022). Chain-of-thought prompting elicits reasoning in large language models. *Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 35*. https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903
findings.correction: No correction needed. Citation is accurate.

findings.title: Brown et al. GPT-3 paper correctly cited
findings.description: The GPT-3 paper citation is correct: Brown et al. (2020), NeurIPS 33, arXiv 2005.14165. The description 'introduced few-shot prompting' is a reasonable characterization.
findings.line_context: Brown, T. B., Mann, B., Ryder, N., Subbiah, M., Kaplan, J., Dhariwal, P., ... Amodei, D. (2020). Language models are few-shot learners. *Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 33*. https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165
findings.correction: No correction needed. Citation is accurate.

findings.title: Meta-Prompting and APE paper citations are correct
findings.description: Module 08 cites Meta-Prompting (arXiv 2401.12954) and APE (arXiv 2211.01910). Both arXiv IDs are correct.
findings.line_context: Meta-Prompting: Enhancing Language Models with Task-Agnostic Scaffolding (https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.12954), Automatic Prompt Engineer (APE) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.01910)
findings.correction: No correction needed. Citations are accurate.

findings.title: CLAUDE.md hierarchy is accurately described
findings.description: Module 02 correctly describes the CLAUDE.md hierarchy: Global (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md), Project (./CLAUDE.md or ./.claude/CLAUDE.md), and Subdirectory (./subdir/CLAUDE.md). The description that 'more specific context augments or overrides broader context' is accurate. The description that CLAUDE.md is 'read at the start of every session' is correct.
findings.line_context: CLAUDE.md is a Markdown file that Claude Code reads at the start of every session.
findings.correction: No correction needed. Description is accurate.

findings.title: settings.json allow/deny model correctly described
findings.description: Module 10 correctly describes the Claude Code permission model: allow list, deny list, deny takes precedence, unmatched actions require human approval. The evaluation order (deny first, then allow, then prompt user) is accurately stated.
findings.line_context: The evaluation logic is: if the requested action matches a deny rule, it is denied, regardless of whether it also matches an allow rule. Deny takes precedence.
findings.correction: No correction needed. The permission model is accurately described.

findings.title: --continue and --resume flags correctly described
findings.description: Module 01 correctly describes `--continue` (resumes most recent session) and `--resume` (lets you select a prior session). These are valid Claude Code CLI flags.
findings.line_context: `--continue` resumes the most recent session, while `--resume` lets you select a prior session.
findings.correction: No correction needed. Both flags are correctly described.

findings.title: Three MCP primitives correctly identified
findings.description: All modules consistently identify the three MCP server-side primitives as Tools, Resources, and Prompts. This is correct per the MCP specification.
findings.line_context: MCP servers expose three core server-side capabilities: Tools, Resources, and Prompts
findings.correction: No correction needed. The three primitives are correctly identified throughout.

findings.title: SSE correctly marked as deprecated with Streamable HTTP as replacement
findings.description: Module 05 correctly states that HTTP+SSE is superseded by Streamable HTTP in the current MCP specification. The deprecation is consistently noted across modules 05, 06, 11.
findings.line_context: The older HTTP+SSE pattern from earlier protocol versions is superseded by Streamable HTTP in the current specification and should not be used in new implementations.
findings.correction: No correction needed. SSE deprecation is correctly described.

findings.title: Extended thinking description is appropriately hedged
findings.description: Module 03 describes extended thinking as 'model-level capability' with 'model modes that allocate additional reasoning budget before final output.' It correctly notes that 'Thinking controls are model- and API-version dependent' and 'it is not a learner-adjustable CLI flag in normal coursework.' This avoids claiming a `--thinking` CLI flag exists (which would be a zero-tolerance violation).
findings.line_context: Thinking controls are model- and API-version dependent (for example, adaptive thinking in newer model families). In Claude Code, reasoning depth is managed by the runtime; it is not a learner-adjustable CLI flag in normal coursework.
findings.correction: No correction needed. The description carefully avoids claiming any CLI flag for thinking and correctly states it is runtime-managed.

findings.title: PRAO acronym is a course invention, clearly labeled as such
findings.description: The PRAO loop (Perceive, Reason, Act, Observe) is presented as 'this course's teaching model' that 'extends the ReAct framing with explicit Perceive and Observe phases.' It is not presented as an industry-standard or Anthropic-official framework. This honest labeling avoids confabulation.
findings.line_context: The PRAO loop is this course's teaching model for agentic AI behavior. It stands for: Perceive, Reason, Act, Observe. It extends the ReAct framing with explicit Perceive and Observe phases.
findings.correction: No correction needed. The PRAO loop is clearly identified as a course-specific teaching model.

findings.title: Triggers field correctly disclaimed as non-native
findings.description: Module 07 explicitly states that the `triggers` field in skill YAML frontmatter is a 'LUXOR project convention -- not a native Claude Code feature' and that 'the triggers field is not parsed or acted upon by the Claude Code runtime.' This matches the project's zero-tolerance rule about triggers.
findings.line_context: triggers: **LUXOR project convention -- not a native Claude Code feature.** The `triggers` field is not parsed or acted upon by the Claude Code runtime.
findings.correction: No correction needed. The disclaimer is clear and accurate.

findings.title: Permission pattern syntax may not match exact Claude Code format
findings.description: Module 10 uses permission patterns like `Read(**)`, `Write(src/**)`, `Bash(npm test)` in settings.json examples. The exact syntax of Claude Code permission patterns should be verified. Claude Code's actual settings.json permissions may use string patterns rather than the `ToolName(pattern)` functional syntax shown. The correct format is tool-name-prefixed strings in the allow/deny arrays.
findings.line_context: "allow": ["Read(**)", "Write(src/**)", "Write(tests/**)", "Bash(npm test)"]
findings.correction: Verify the exact permission pattern syntax against current Claude Code documentation. The patterns shown (e.g., `Read(**)`, `Bash(npm test)`) appear to be a plausible representation of the actual format, but the exact syntax should be confirmed. If Claude Code uses a different format (e.g., plain strings like `Bash(npm test:*)` or different glob patterns), the examples should be updated.

findings.title: Agent tool and run_in_background accurately described for multi-agent
findings.description: Module 09's Mermaid diagram shows 'Agent tool run_in_background:true' for dispatching parallel background agents. This is consistent with Claude Code's actual sub-agent mechanism where the Task/Agent tool can be used with background execution. The description that agents write results to files which are then read by the parent is a valid pattern.
findings.line_context: PARENT -->|"Agent tool\nrun_in_background:true"| B1
findings.correction: No correction needed. The pattern described is consistent with Claude Code's sub-agent capabilities.

findings.title: No fabricated features or non-existent APIs detected
findings.description: A comprehensive scan of all 12 modules found no instances of fabricated Claude Code features, non-existent API parameters, or aspirational features presented as current. The modules avoid the five zero-tolerance violations: no `--thinking` flag, no `--context` flag, no `/memory` command, SSE correctly deprecated, exactly three MCP primitives stated.
findings.line_context: N/A - absence finding
findings.correction: No correction needed.

findings.title: Kojima et al. zero-shot reasoners paper correctly cited
findings.description: Module 03 correctly cites Kojima et al. (2022) 'Large language models are zero-shot reasoners' with the correct arXiv ID 2205.11916 and venue (NeurIPS 35). The informal description 'The Let's think step by step paper' is accurate.
findings.line_context: Kojima, T., Gu, S. S., Reid, M., Matsuo, Y., & Iwasawa, Y. (2022). Large language models are zero-shot reasoners. *Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 35*. https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.11916
findings.correction: No correction needed. Citation is accurate.

findings.title: MCP SDK code examples use correct API surface
findings.description: Module 06's TypeScript SDK examples use `McpServer` from `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/mcp.js`, `StdioServerTransport` from `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/stdio.js`, and Zod for schema definition. The `server.tool()`, `server.resource()`, and `server.prompt()` APIs shown are consistent with the high-level McpServer API in the TypeScript SDK.
findings.line_context: import { McpServer } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/mcp.js";
findings.correction: No correction needed. The SDK API surface shown is accurate for the McpServer high-level API.

findings.title: Tool result structure with isError flag correctly described
findings.description: Module 06 correctly describes MCP tool results as a content array with an optional `isError` flag. The structure shown (`{ content: [{ type: 'text', text: '...' }], isError: true }`) is accurate per the MCP specification.
findings.line_context: The MCP protocol defines a tool result as a content array with an optional `isError` flag.
findings.correction: No correction needed. The tool result structure is accurately described.

key_issues: ACC-002: claude_desktop_config.json referenced instead of settings.json for Claude Code (medium)
ACC-017: Permission pattern syntax should be verified against actual Claude Code format (medium)

commentary.overall_assessment: The 12 modules demonstrate high technical accuracy across all categories. No critical errors were found. The content successfully avoids all five zero-tolerance violations defined in the project CLAUDE.md (no --thinking flag, no --context flag, no /memory command, SSE correctly deprecated, exactly three MCP primitives). All academic citations checked are correct. The most significant finding is ACC-002, where Module 05 references claude_desktop_config.json (a Claude Desktop file) instead of settings.json (the Claude Code configuration file). This is an internal inconsistency since other modules correctly reference settings.json.
strongest_areas: MCP protocol accuracy: Three primitives, SSE deprecation, transport mechanisms, SDK APIs all correct
Academic citations: All arXiv IDs, venues, and author lists verified correct
CLAUDE.md behavior: Correctly described as read at session start, hierarchy accurate
Zero-tolerance compliance: No fabricated CLI flags, commands, or features
Extended thinking: Carefully hedged to avoid claiming non-existent CLI flags
areas_for_verification: ACC-002: Replace claude_desktop_config.json with settings.json in M05
ACC-001: Verify --output-format json against current Claude Code CLI docs
ACC-017: Verify exact permission pattern syntax (Read(**), Write(src/**), etc.) against docs
